skill.name: worktree-manager-skill
skill.description: Guides AI assistants through Git worktree creation, listing, removal, and troubleshooting for parallel development environments.
skill.summary: Manage Git worktrees, isolated ports, service status, and cleanup workflows for parallel development.
skill.author: EGAdams

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many command, URL, environment file, and weak-crypto patterns, but the reviewed content is Markdown guidance for Git worktree slash commands rather than executable code. No prompt injection, malicious network destination, credential exfiltration, or real cryptographic use was found. The residual risk is medium because the workflow can create and remove worktrees, install dependencies, start services, edit environment files, and kill processes.

medium_findings.title: Operational File and Process Changes
medium_findings.description: The skill intentionally routes users to slash commands that create worktree directories, install dependencies, start services, stop services, kill processes, and remove worktrees. This is legitimate for a worktree manager, but mistakes in branch selection or command implementation could delete files or stop the wrong process.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced lines explicitly describe dependency installation, service startup, process termination, and worktree removal. The intent is legitimate, but the operations have meaningful filesystem and process impact.

medium_findings.title: Manual Cleanup Guidance Can Be Destructive
medium_findings.description: Troubleshooting guidance includes manual process killing and recursive directory removal for orphaned worktrees. The commands are scoped to worktree paths, but they still require user confirmation and careful path validation before use.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The lines contain direct manual cleanup instructions with kill and recursive removal semantics. The examples are bounded, so this is an operational risk rather than evidence of malicious behavior.

low_findings.title: External Command Findings Are Mostly Documentation Examples
low_findings.description: Static analysis identified many command-like strings, but they are Markdown examples for slash commands and Git troubleshooting. No executable script file or automatic shell execution path was found in the reviewed skill files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The evidence is contained in Markdown prose and fenced examples. The skill declares Bash availability, but these lines do not implement executable code.

low_findings.title: Localhost URL Findings Are Example Outputs
low_findings.description: Hardcoded URL findings point to localhost dashboard, server, client, and configuration examples. These are local development endpoints, not external exfiltration destinations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Every reviewed URL uses localhost with development ports. No remote host, webhook, or third-party network target was found.

low_findings.title: Environment File References Are Configuration Guidance
low_findings.description: The skill references .env files and worktree-specific settings as part of port and service isolation. No evidence found that secrets are read, printed, copied to remote systems, or exfiltrated.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The .env references are configuration examples for local ports and database isolation. The reviewed files do not show credential access or data transfer.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Static Hits Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The reviewed lines flagged as weak cryptography are normal prose, headings, or Markdown content. No hashing, encryption, cryptographic library calls, or security-sensitive algorithm selection was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited locations do not contain cryptographic operations. The static pattern appears to have matched ordinary words or documentation context.

dangerous_patterns.title: Worktree Removal Workflow
dangerous_patterns.description: Removal guidance includes stopping services, killing processes, removing Git worktrees, and cleaning directories. This should be gated by explicit user intent and verified branch names.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The documentation clearly describes deletion and cleanup behavior. The pattern is expected for this skill but still security-relevant.

dangerous_patterns.title: Manual Kill and Recursive Remove Examples
dangerous_patterns.description: Troubleshooting examples include force-killing a PID and removing an orphaned worktree directory. These actions can cause data loss if the target is wrong.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The examples are explicit and operationally risky. The paths are scoped to worktrees, which lowers the likelihood of malicious intent.

content.value_statement: Parallel branch work often creates port conflicts, stale services, and unclear cleanup steps. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through worktree creation, listing, removal, and troubleshooting.
actual_capabilities: Selects the correct worktree slash command for create, list, and remove requests.
Explains branch name and port offset inputs before creating a worktree.
Reports local dashboard, server, client, and port details after setup.
Guides status checks for running services, process IDs, and port conflicts.
Provides cleanup workflows for stale, orphaned, or unused worktrees.
Documents port allocation and per-worktree configuration patterns.
limitations: Requires the referenced slash commands to exist in the host environment.
Does not implement the worktree commands itself.
Cannot guarantee safety of external command implementations it calls.
Assumes a project layout with trees, local services, and port-based development.

use_cases.title: Create isolated feature environments
use_cases.description: Create a worktree for a feature branch with unique local ports and service access details.

use_cases.title: Audit active worktrees
use_cases.description: List active worktrees, running services, ports, process IDs, and access URLs in one overview.

use_cases.title: Clean stale development environments
use_cases.description: Remove old worktrees, stop related services, and resolve orphaned directories or port conflicts.

prompt_templates.title: List my worktrees
prompt_templates.prompt: Show me all current worktrees and tell me which ones are running.

prompt_templates.title: Create a feature worktree
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a worktree for the feature-auth branch and show me the access URLs.

prompt_templates.title: Create with a fixed port offset
prompt_templates.prompt: Set up a worktree for hotfix-login with port offset 4, then summarize the server and client ports.

prompt_templates.title: Diagnose and clean conflicts
prompt_templates.prompt: Check my worktrees for stopped services, port conflicts, and orphaned directories. Ask before removing anything.

output_examples.output: The assistant chooses the create workflow, confirms the branch name, runs the configured worktree command, and reports the new local URLs and ports.
The assistant lists each worktree, its path, service status, process identifiers, local URLs, and any port conflicts that need attention.

output_examples.input: Remove the old checkout for feature-auth.
output_examples.output: The assistant confirms the target worktree, uses the removal workflow, then reports stopped services, cleanup results, and any remaining issues.

best_practices: Confirm the branch or worktree name before creating or removing anything.
Use explicit port offsets when several environments must run together.
List worktrees before cleanup so running services and conflicts are visible.
anti_patterns: Do not remove worktrees when the user only asked to inspect status.
Do not run manual recursive deletion without validating the exact worktree path.
Do not assume localhost ports are free without checking active worktrees first.

faq.question: What does this skill manage?
faq.answer: It manages Git worktree workflows for creating, listing, removing, and troubleshooting parallel development environments.

faq.question: Does it include executable scripts?
faq.answer: No. The reviewed files are Markdown instructions that call expected slash commands in the host environment.

faq.question: Why is the risk level medium?
faq.answer: The skill can guide filesystem changes, dependency installation, service startup, and process cleanup. These actions need careful confirmation.

faq.question: Can it prevent port conflicts?
faq.answer: It documents port offsets and status checks that help avoid conflicts between local worktrees.

faq.question: Can it delete branches?
faq.answer: The documentation says removing a worktree does not delete the Git branch. Users should still verify command behavior locally.

faq.question: Which tools can use this skill?
faq.answer: The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
